periods.objective: Identify and name the parts of a circle.
periods.workedExamples: 5-1, Examples 1-3 — Identify Parts of a Circle from a Diagram; Find Arc Length Given Radius and Central Angle; Find Sector Area
periods.notes: Core circle vocabulary: parts identification, arc length, and sector area.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 5a with one retrieval prompt naming parts of a circle from a labeled diagram.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 5-1, Examples 1-3 to develop: identify radius, diameter, chord, arc, and sector from a diagram, compute arc length from radius and central angle using (θ/360) · 2πr, and find sector area using (θ/360) · πr².
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 5-1, Examples 1-3: label circle parts and compute arc lengths and sector areas; teacher checks vocabulary and formula application.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on circle_parts_arc_sector: identification, arc length, and sector area problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one arc length calculation and one sector area calculation.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with identifying circle parts and computing arcs and sectors.

periods.objective: Apply circle vocabulary and arc/sector formulas to real-world problems.
periods.workedExamples: 5-1, Examples 4-5 — Relationship Between Radius and Diameter; Real-World: Clock Face Arc Measurements
periods.notes: Radius-diameter relationship and real-world clock face arc application.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 5b with one retrieval prompt on the relationship between radius, diameter, and circumference.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 5-1, Examples 4-5 to develop: compute radius, circumference, and area from a given diameter, and apply arc length and sector area formulas to a clock face where the minute hand sweeps from 12 to 4, recognizing the 120° central angle.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 5-1, Examples 4-5: convert between radius and diameter and solve real-world arc/sector problems; teacher checks formula selection and unit consistency.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on circle_parts_arc_sector: radius-diameter conversions and real-world arc measurement problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one radius-diameter-circumference computation and one clock face arc problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with applying circle formulas to real-world contexts.

periods.objective: State and apply the inscribed angle theorem.
periods.workedExamples: 5-2, Examples 1-3 — Find Inscribed Angle Given Intercepted Arc; Find Central Angle Given Intercepted Arc; Inscribed Angle in a Semicircle = 90°
periods.notes: Inscribed angle theorem, central angle relationships, and semicircle right-angle property.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 5d with one retrieval prompt on the relationship between an inscribed angle and its intercepted arc.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 5-2, Examples 1-3 to develop: the inscribed angle is half the intercepted arc, find inscribed and central angles from arc measures, and apply the special case that an inscribed angle in a semicircle is 90°.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 5-2, Examples 1-3: compute inscribed and central angles from arcs and verify the half-angle relationship; teacher checks theorem application.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on inscribed_angle_theorem: inscribed angle, central angle, and semicircle problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one inscribed angle calculation and one semicircle right-angle identification.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with the inscribed angle theorem.

periods.objective: Solve for missing angle measures in circles with multiple inscribed angles.
periods.workedExamples: 5-2, Examples 4-5 — Find Missing Angle Measures with Multiple Inscribed Angles; Inscribed Angle Theorem Application
periods.notes: Multi-inscribed-angle problems and complex inscribed angle theorem applications.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 5e with one retrieval prompt on how two inscribed angles that intercept the same arc are related.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 5-2, Examples 4-5 to develop: solve for missing angles when multiple inscribed angles share arcs, apply the inscribed angle theorem in layered configurations, and chain multiple angle relationships in a single figure.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 5-2, Examples 4-5: trace intercepted arcs and compute multiple inscribed angles in one figure; teacher checks arc-angle tracing.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on inscribed_angle_theorem: complex multi-angle problems within circle figures.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one multi-inscribed-angle problem requiring two steps of reasoning.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with multi-inscribed-angle problems.

nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by recent objective evidence and misconception patterns.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems that combine current objectives with prior units.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes due SRS items and submits one corrected-error reflection.

periods.objective: Find chord length given distance from center and radius.
periods.workedExamples: 5-3, Examples 1-3 — Find Chord Length Given Distance from Center and Radius; Congruent Chords Are Equidistant from Center; Find Arc Measure Given Chord
periods.notes: Core chord properties: chord length from Pythagorean theorem, congruent chord equidistance, and arc-chord relationships.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 5f with one retrieval prompt on the perpendicular distance from a chord to the center and the right triangle it forms.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 5-3, Examples 1-3 to develop: compute chord length from the distance to center and radius using (c/2)² + d² = r², apply the congruent-chord equidistant property, and relate arc measure to chord properties.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 5-3, Examples 1-3: draw radius-to-chord diagrams and compute lengths; teacher checks diagram setup and Pythagorean application.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on chord_properties: chord length and congruent chord problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one chord length calculation and one congruent chord reasoning problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with chord properties.

periods.objective: Apply chord properties including perpendicular bisector and real-world problems.
periods.workedExamples: 5-3, Examples 4-5 — Perpendicular Bisector of Chord Passes Through Center; Real-World: Finding Width of a Circular Pond
periods.notes: Perpendicular bisector property and real-world circular pond width application.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 5g with one retrieval prompt on what the perpendicular bisector of a chord tells us about the center of the circle.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 5-3, Examples 4-5 to develop: verify that the perpendicular bisector of a chord passes through the center by finding midpoints and checking center coordinates, and apply the chord-distance-radius relationship to find the radius of a circular pond given a bridge chord.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 5-3, Examples 4-5: use perpendicular bisector reasoning and chord-distance formulas in real-world contexts; teacher checks geometric reasoning.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on chord_properties: perpendicular bisector and real-world chord problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one perpendicular bisector reasoning problem and one real-world chord application.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with the perpendicular bisector property and real-world chord problems.

periods.notes: Mathematical practices focus: choosing between arc, chord, tangent, and secant relationships; error analysis in circle theorems.

nonInstructionArtifact.groupTask: Mathematical practices focus: choosing between arc, chord, tangent, and secant relationships; error analysis in circle theorems.
nonInstructionArtifact.sourceWorkedExamples: Use the prior instruction periods named in the notes as the problem bank.
nonInstructionArtifact.deliverable: Group solution poster or shared explanation with one strategy comparison.
nonInstructionArtifact.facilitationNotes: Assign roles for theorem identification, diagram analysis, computation, and explanation.
nonInstructionArtifact.completionCriteria: Correct method, accurate result, clear justification, and response to peer question.

periods.notes: Mid-unit review across 5a-5g. Covers circle vocabulary, arc/sector, inscribed angle theorem, and chord properties.

nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by recent objective evidence and misconception patterns.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems that combine inscribed angle theorem with chord properties.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes review problem set and submits one self-identified area for improvement.

periods.objective: Prove that a tangent line is perpendicular to the radius at the point of tangency.
periods.workedExamples: 5-4, Examples 1-2 — Prove Tangent Is Perpendicular to Radius; Find Segment Length Using Tangent from External Point
periods.notes: Tangent perpendicularity proof and tangent segment lengths from external points.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 5h with one retrieval prompt defining what a tangent line is and how it relates to a circle.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 5-4, Examples 1-2 to develop: prove the tangent is perpendicular to the radius at the point of tangency by contradiction, and find segment lengths when a tangent from an external point is drawn using the Pythagorean theorem.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 5-4, Examples 1-2: apply tangent-radius perpendicularity and compute segment lengths; teacher checks perpendicularity reasoning.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on tangent_secant_properties: perpendicularity proofs and segment length problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one tangent perpendicularity reasoning problem and one segment length calculation.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with tangent perpendicularity and segment lengths.

periods.objective: Apply tangent-tangent angles and secant-secant segment relationships.
periods.workedExamples: 5-4, Examples 3-4 — Tangent-Tangent Angle from Intercepted Arcs; Secant-Secant Segment Length Relationship
periods.notes: Tangent-tangent angle formula and secant-secant product relationship.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 5i with one retrieval prompt on how to find the angle formed by two tangent lines from an external point.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 5-4, Examples 3-4 to develop: compute tangent-tangent angles from the difference of intercepted arcs, and apply the secant-secant segment length relationship (external × whole = external × whole).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 5-4, Examples 3-4: compute tangent-tangent angles and set up secant-secant equations; teacher checks arc identification and equation setup.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on tangent_secant_properties: tangent-tangent angle and secant-secant segment problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one tangent-tangent angle calculation and one secant-secant segment problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with tangent angles and secant relationships.

periods.objective: Apply tangent-secant power of a point to real-world problems.
periods.workedExamples: 5-4, Examples 5-6 — Tangent-Secant Power of a Point; Real-World: Satellite Tangent to Earth's Surface
periods.notes: Power of a point for tangent-secant configurations and satellite real-world application.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 5j with one retrieval prompt on the power of a point theorem for tangent-secant configurations.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 5-4, Examples 5-6 to develop: apply the tangent-secant power of a point theorem (tangent² = external × full secant), and model a satellite tangent to Earth's surface using the Pythagorean theorem with tangent-radius perpendicularity.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 5-4, Examples 5-6: set up and solve power-of-a-point equations and satellite geometry problems; teacher checks segment identification.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on tangent_secant_properties: tangent-secant power of a point and real-world problems.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one power-of-a-point calculation and one real-world tangent problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with power of a point and real-world tangent applications.

periods.objective: Write and graph equations of circles in standard form.
periods.workedExamples: 5-5, Examples 1-2 — Write Equation of Circle Given Center and Radius; Identify Center and Radius from Equation
periods.notes: Core standard form: writing equations and identifying center/radius.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 5k with one retrieval prompt on the standard form of a circle equation (x − h)² + (y − k)² = r².
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 5-5, Examples 1-2 to develop: write a circle equation from a given center and radius, and identify center and radius from a given equation by matching to standard form.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 5-5, Examples 1-2: write and interpret circle equations in standard form; teacher checks center-radius identification and equation structure.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on circle_equations: writing and identifying circle equations in standard form.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one write-the-equation problem and one identify-center-and-radius problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with circle equations in standard form.

periods.objective: Write circle equations from graphs and complete the square to convert forms.
periods.workedExamples: 5-5, Examples 3-4 — Write Equation from Graph; Complete the Square to Convert General Form
periods.notes: Writing equations from graphs and completing the square for form conversion.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 5l with one retrieval prompt on completing the square for a single-variable quadratic expression.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 5-5, Examples 3-4 to develop: write a circle equation from a graph by reading the center and radius, and complete the square in both x and y to convert a general-form circle equation to standard form.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 5-5, Examples 3-4: write equations from graphs and complete the square for x and y terms; teacher checks algebraic steps.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on circle_equations: graph-to-equation conversion and completing the square.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one write-from-graph problem and one completing-the-square conversion.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with writing circle equations from graphs and completing the square.

periods.objective: Find circle equations through points and graph from general form.
periods.workedExamples: 5-5, Examples 5-6 — Find Equation of Circle Through Three Points; Graph a Circle from Its Equation
periods.notes: Three-point circle equations and graphing from general form.

dailyPhases.warmUp: Activate prior knowledge for 5m with one retrieval prompt on how substituting a point into the general form x² + y² + Dx + Ey + F = 0 produces one equation in three unknowns.
dailyPhases.conceptDevelopment: Use 5-5, Examples 5-6 to develop: find the equation of a circle passing through three points by setting up and solving a system of three equations, and graph a circle from its equation by identifying center and radius and plotting key points.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete scaffolded problems modeled on 5-5, Examples 5-6: substitute points into the general form and solve the resulting system, then graph circles; teacher checks system setup and graphing accuracy.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Students complete aligned practice on circle_equations: three-point circle problems and graphing from equations.
dailyPhases.assessment: Exit ticket: one three-point circle equation problem and one graph-from-equation problem.
dailyPhases.capReflection: CAP reflection: Name the strategy that helped you persist with three-point circle equations and graphing.

nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by recent objective evidence and misconception patterns.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems that combine current objectives with prior units.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes due SRS items and submits one corrected-error reflection.

nonInstructionArtifact.remediationGrouping: Group students by recent objective evidence and misconception patterns.
nonInstructionArtifact.extensionOptions: Offer synthesis problems that combine current objectives with prior units.
nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Student completes review problem set and submits one self-identified area for improvement.
